sentences.text: My name is Rachel Drabkin.
Drabkin, D-R-A-B-K-I-N.
And when were you born?
I'm born the 9 of March, '21, 1921.
I'm born in Vilna.
In Poland.
My father's name was Leibel Kaplan.
He was born-- I don't know exactly the age because-- when it was the Germans coming, then he was at 45 years old.
My mother's name was Peshe, and she was the same age with my father, 45 years old, when the Germans came.
Chaiken.
This I don't know.
Now, my sister was Icei.
I-- I-C-E-I, Icei, Icei Kaplan.
And my brother's name was Luskieh, Luskieh Kaplan.
Icei was born-- I was born in '21.
She was born 1924.
And Luskieh was born four years later.
There was 19-- I think it's 1930 he was born.
In Vilna.
Before the war.
From the start, I was living-- they called-- the street was called [NON-ENGLISH].
There was opposite soccer place.
And there was living for a long time.
And the school what I was going there, there was a high school.
And before that, I think there was on-- we had a shop on [NON-ENGLISH].
And there was near my primary school.
There was primary school that was calling [NON-ENGLISH] Number Eight.
And then-- and after that I went to the high school, what the high school was called-- the name for the high school is Epstein.
There was a make to only two forms.
One I remember, one was [NON-ENGLISH] 18, number 18.
And this was a place where there was opposite a market that's called the Holtz Market.
And that's where was living before we going into the ghetto.
That was but four or five years there.
Yes.
There was three bedroom.
There was unusually for this time of-- the place for like Vilna.
And very comfortable home.

And the kitchen-- there used to be, not like we have now, there used to be, I suppose, an oven made from-- from-- what they call-- from the-- like you might put-- a big oven, and my mother used to put the cakes there, the-- the-- all the food to such a big.
On and the top of the oven, she keep the always for Pesach [NON-ENGLISH] dishes.
This was the place, the hiding place there.
And if you feeled a little very cold, there was a place where you could sit down and warm yourself up because the warmth come up through the-- [LAUGHS] through the oven to there to lie down.
No.
I never helped my mother cooking.
When-- I always, always say, mama, I want to go into the kitchen to help you.
She said, don't worry.
When you marry, I teach you.
That's true.
I had one with my sister together.
And my brother had separately.
It was a living room.
Can call a living room.
There was not a bed in it.
It was a living room.
My father used to work in this living room.
There was a table and chairs and--
And, we had-- we didn't have a bathroom in the home.
Just, we had a business somewhere else.
And there was an empty room.
So we make a bath, a big bath like-- and a kettle with to warm the water on there.
We was have the baths.
Not far away.
I had my aunt-- my aunties.
They all lived in Vilna.
It's not far one from the other.
And I had an auntie what was living together with us for a long time because the business was together be there.
And the-- and her daughter was brought up mostly from my parents.
She was living all the time with us together.
My mother's sister.
Her name was Merle.
And she had, like, the cousin, which one she's not anymore alive.
She passed away.
And we was living all the time with her together.
And then I had-- my mother had another sister she called Malka, what she lived in Vilna too.
And she had a very big business-- [NON-ENGLISH].
There's what they called.
And-- and she had two children.
And one from there was survivor with me together in all the concentration camp.
We survived together.
She's now in Israel.

What I can tell from my home life-- we had a-- in compare from the time from Vilna and the poor-- there was so much poor people-- we had a very good home life because it doesn't matter how bad it could be at home.
We children never felt.
For us was everything.
Maybe my parents didn't have for themselves so much.
Just for us was always everything.
Yes, my mother kept a very kosher home.
She was not religious, but she was very, very kosher.
And all the yontifs, she keep them, you know.
And she was very particular in this part from tradition.
yontif and Shabbat.
Only yontif.
Only yontif like Rosh Hashanah.
Not always.
Some things we had-- we invited them.
Not my mother didn't go to them because they wasn't kosher enough for her.
She couldn't.
Pesach Seder-- we had-- we was with the family together.
Like she had two sisters.
She was the one which was a very good cook, my mother.
So she invited them, and we had the yontif together.
[INAUDIBLE] what she makes for Pesach.
This was very good.
And-- but she used to make-- I don't know if you know about it.
It's called a [INAUDIBLE].
You don't know.
This, you have your potatoes.
You make them like a-- like a cake.
You put them in a dish and bake them.
I still make them today.
Yeah, I make a lot with my mother.
Even she didn't teach me, oh, but I still remember from her a lot of things to do it.
And I'm doing it.
From school, I went first to-- the school was a Polish school, a government school from the start.
They made them sixth grade in the government.
School, there was near where I was living not far away.
This is called [NON-ENGLISH] because on the corner, we had our shop, like a delicatessen all together, like a mixed business.
And not far away from there was the shul what I was walking to the shul.
And then I makes this class.
And then-- then from there I had a lot of friends, a lot of friends.
And we just finished the school, and we went to get at the high school.
And I wasn't very, very good in high school.
I make maybe two-- two forms.
That's all.
And then my mother said, look, you-- if you can't learn like this, we do everything for you, even to have teachers home and teach us to help us to go to school.
So she said, you know, you have to learn a profession.
You can't be like this.
You need a profession.
So I went to professional school.
And the school was called the [NON-ENGLISH].
And then, I didn't finish.
I think because all the war broke out.
Or something.
Something was there.
But I-- I didn't finish the school.
And I went there for near a couple years which as little as there was for four years the course of there to finish.
And-- and then I meet a lot of new friends.
Like high school is a difference.
And there is difference.
I meet a lot of friends.
And these friends from this school who was all-- all my life till the-- till the ghetto.
My father used to make the top for the shoes.
I don't know what they call them.
Like, not the shoemaker, just the tops for the shoe.
The leather part, he would making.
And the top from the shoes.
And this was after he had the mixed business.
First he had--
This after.
He never was in the mixed business.
This was only my mother's.
And he was-- he was working separately.
There was-- he wasn't in the business.
And then the-- the factory what they have for the shoes, he didn't work so good.
So he bought a shoe shop himself.
Which runned-- was very bad because then we went broke.
He-- he took the shop over.
Then somebody is handing money, you know, to somebody.
He had to pay off.
Then we close it then.
And my mother used to stay always in the business.
No.
I come for [INAUDIBLE] but didn't help.
No.
Till Australia never worked.
Yiddish.
Not really.
No.
With my parents, I speak only Yiddish.
But I speaks Polish and Russian and a little bit of Hebrew then.
Not much.
I mean, Vilna is very knowing-- there's a [INAUDIBLE] not a lot of people was knowing about the Hebrew because they learn in [INAUDIBLE], and this is not, not a lot about it.
There was a Jewish-- there was a Jewish commandant sent in for the city, you know.
And-- I definitely didn't go there much because I was young, and we was looking always to find, like, young people.
So I went to the organizations like Betar Hashomer Hatzair and things like that what was more interesting.
With my friends, we used to-- all week we was busy.
Couldn't go because of school.
And Friday night, we used to go out.
There was one street they called [NON-ENGLISH].
All the youngster for the-- was living-- doesn't matter where they are.
They used to walk.
There was no transport.
We used to walk down to the street.
And all-- we meet the boys and girls.
Everyone was talking and laughing.
There was the one cafeteria there.
You can-- if you had the money to go in and buy a cup of tea and a piece of cake and sit down.
And it was a very, very interesting life.
And except this, we have the organizations in the night then.
And the theater was.
The theatre was going most of them from the school, like, the providing a play.
So this-- this theatre was opposite the school that I was learning.
There was a four [INAUDIBLE] they call the Radota, the Radota Theatre.
And when I was there, to see Vilna, I saw it.
It's still there.
Yes, I've been when my mother used to-- there was-- there was a garden they called Ver Medina garden.
And they used to-- my-- in summer, there was an open.
And they used to have beautiful plays and music.
And my mother-- because I was the older one and not to take the youngster to.
She said, you know what, you, I take you.
You go, you see how beautiful it is.
And I remember there was a beautiful garden.
It's still there when I was visiting Vilna.
Yes, I went with my parents.
Oh, yes.
Yeah, my mother was very educated because she was-- she was born in Russia.
And she finished the school, the high school there.
And she was a very clever woman.
She was-- she read a lot, and she knows a lot.
I tell you not much because there really then there was-- there was nothing, no Jewish.
Like I was Jewish-- I had my Jewish company.
I had nothing to do with non-Jewish world.
Maybe my mother, yes, because the business.
But amidst children, I never had non-Jewish friends.
Yeah, there were 75,000 Jewish people in Vilna.
It was more like Poland.
I tell you, anti-Semitism was very, very big.
It was in 1938, '39.
It was terrible.
There was a university that start-- started to put the people on the right and left, you know, for like Jewish and no-Jewish.
But for me personally, I didn't feel it nothing because I didn't-- never had something to do it in.
Yes.
You were all-- they all felt.
They all felt because you had them-- like in Polish, Zyd, Zyd, out!
You know, like, in Polish, out!
So you had them always.
If you pass-- if you go up the street or you see that you look like Jewish, you something you get tell off, you know.
But-- we didn't-- didn't think about it.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember when the Russians come to our place in 1940.
So my mother said, the Russian-- that's the vilest people in the world because she was born there, and she had such a bad experience from them.
But she had the whole family.
We helped them from Vilna to Russia.
So my mother said, only no, the Russians.
Better the Germans.
Just not the Russians.
Who knows because my father was in the fields for the Germans.
So you said, the Germans, if you working, they do for you everything.
Who was thinking that they come and do such a-- things to us.
So we--
Most of it.
The non-Jewish?
No.
Approximately September.
I think in this time, they come in-- the Nazis in 1941-- maybe before September.
In 1941, yes.
And a couple months later, two or three months later, they-- they put us in the ghettos.
I was-- I-- when the-- when the German war broke out, I wasn't home.
There were-- I had an uncle in Lithuania.
And in 1940 when the Russians came, they opened the border between Vilna and Lithuania.
So my father had a brother.
I took my brother and went there for holidays because he lived in a smaller place.
And I stayed there.
Suddenly we hear that the Germans are coming in.
So I start-- I said, the Germans coming.
The war.
We are going back home.
So I took my brother, and we was-- a part of it was going by train, and then we couldn't because the Germans start to come up and bombed.
So me with my brother was hiding under the train.
So when I-- when they stopped the bombs, everybody was dead around us.
Only not me with my brother.
So when we went out, we were start to walking to Vilna.
And when you come to Vilna, you have to show papers.
But we cannot pull the papers because we're Jewish.
We know that it's not good-- not good for them.
So when the Communists said, where are you?
Who you?
I said I'm Polish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And then they let us into Vilna, and I come back home.
Then I find still my mother with my father.
My sister wasn't already there.
My sister-- we had a girl living with us.
She's from a very small-- very small town.
She went to school.
So she lived with us.
And when the Germans came, she said to my sister, you know what, what are you going to stay in the city?
I live in a holidays place.
Let's go.
Come and stay with us.
So my mother let her go.
And she never came back.
Just few-- we sent some people, Polish people there, where they was living, would know about it.
So they bring us this.
This family where she was staying, this-- when the Germans arrived, they said they are Communists.
They are Communists.
So the first family what they took to shoot, there was the family where my sister were there.
And we never saw her.
Never have heard.
And how did life change in Vilna after the Germans came and occupied?
I tell there was no life to tell because how long they stay in the German, they put us right away in the ghetto.
At first, they was looking.
Then they come, the Germans, in Vilna.
They took first the old men to try to take them first away to shoot them out.
So my father was very lucky.
We was living in a big yard, and he could run over another-- to another building, over the windows, or run away.
So it was like he didn't give in to get to the ghetto.
And to get for I or my brother or my father or my mother, we went in.
And did you or your family ever attempt to run away?
We had-- they used, in 1939, that a load of Poland arrived to Vilna because the Germans were there.
And they used to come, one, to make some handbags to my father because he had the machine.
So he come off and said to my father, you know what, I'm going to Shanghai.
We are allowed to go.
Are you want me your older daughter to go with me?
My father said, what?
What?
You take a girl from me to go like that with you?
No way.
This was the only opportunity what I had, could stay alive, not to go to Germany.
And your parents weren't offered.
He only offered to take you.
Yes, only-- you couldn't.
He take-- could take me like a bride because he was a single man.
The conditions there was like that.
Like I-- person like me.
We was in one big room.
There used to be a high school there, they call [NON-ENGLISH].
And in this room was my mother's three sisters and another-- another two families.
So we was altogether, six families in a one room.
And everyone of us had two children then.
So we all stayed in the same room.
And the facility-- there was one little oven in the passage.
And everybody has his turn when he can cook because six families together.
And you have nothing in there really.
The-- in the ghetto they make theatres, you know.
You could go if you would wanted.
And this-- they tried to get to provide the life like a normal life-- the people not to feel that they are so been hurt, you know, to go on with your life.
But in other way, some of you was worse hurt.
Like I was hurt from my sister's side.
Then other people lost already half of the family because there-- there was two ghettos.
There was ghetto number one, and there was ghetto number two.
Ghetto-- who come into ghetto number one, they all went to Ponary to shot.
And the-- I was in ghetto number two so I was lucky to stay alive.
And the life in ghetto, we had something too because my-- my father was working for Gebietskommissar Murer.
People know exactly, you know, been over there.
And he was working there like-- like a shoemaker.
And he used to bring some leather, what was left over-- he'd pinch from there-- and bring them to the ghetto, and he sold them.
So we had the money to-- to live.
So compared to other ones, we had in-- in food we was very, very good.
Very good.
Even I helped-- helped my-- my husband's family there because they didn't have so much, not so good in the ghettos.
And how did-- what happened when you had to move from your house to the ghetto?
From-- from my house in the ghetto?
Into the ghetto.
Into the ghetto.
Nothing.
Whatever somebody could carry in your hand.
And you left everything.
How much you can take it in you hand?
Nothing.
So we left everything there.
All right, my mother took a pot and a kettle.
You know how it is.
She said, we're always the smartest there was.
If not the smartest, we would [INAUDIBLE] might be in Russia.
But how can you leave a home?
My mother said, how can you leave a home?
I've had all this all my life.
I could not going to leave everything there.
And did you get much time from the time they said you have to run, you have to leave?
After-- I don't-- this I can't remember.
Not-- not really much time.
They didn't give you weeks.
That's time for area.
Now you have to move it.
So maybe there was a couple days because he couldn't come when said go to prepare ourselves to go out.
Oh, the ghetto, there was a quarter of the town where the Jewish population was living, most of them there.
And that's where they took you.
Took to there.
Yeah, they make a fence around.
I mean, not a fence-- just they blocked up the windows which was going out from the ghetto.
They blocked up with bricks.
Not to-- you're not to-- you can't go out there.
You know, you can't see there.
Only what is in the ghetto.
There was, I don't know exactly, maybe 10 or 15 streets like that.
Something like that.
They were small streets.
How I find it when we arrived in the ghetto, the Gebietskommissar from the ghetto, provided with the ghetto, he had really give you the places to live.
He really give you.
And because of my father, he was work in such a proper way, very important place with the Germans.
So we had a good place.
There was the Jewish commander was on this part of the yard where I was living.
I had-- we had-- we was four girls and four boys.
Everybody of us had a boyfriend.
Like my husband had a girlfriend.
I had a boyfriend.
Nobody turn up in the ghetto.
Only me and my husband.
Everybody was killed.
So I was going-- I had-- I was very close.
We was in the same company with my husband.
So I was going to him.
And he used to come to me.
And I'm going so long till I fall in love with him.
And this was the company.
And his brother, his sister-in-law was there, and the family.
Most of them, they keep the company most of them with your family.
You didn't look for new friends because when I went in the ghetto, I was 22 years old.
I had all my friends for years and years before.
So you didn't look for new friends in the ghetto.
Not all of them.
Only my husband from the friends.
None of the others.
We hear news when are still-- before the German arrive in Vilna because in 1939, they run away to Vilna.
So then they came and told us, that the Germans going to kill the Jews.
We didn't-- we didn't believe it.
Like my-- my father said, what?
The German, if you work for them, they wouldn't do to you nothing.
Did you get mail?
Letters?
Letters, no.
Newspaper-- there was a Yiddish paper.
And I think in Vilna get.
There was because there was a lot of young, very clever-- he was writing hisself, you know.
But he had even books there, from there.
Like the song what we were singing in Vilna was Hirsh Glik, his name.
He wrote this song.
And this song is now in the whole world in all kind of languages.
Yes, I can.
[CLEARS THROAT] Pardon me.
[SINGING IN YIDDISH] Zog nit keyn mol az du geyst dem letstn veg, himlen blayene farshteln bloye teg.
Kumen vet nokh undzer oysgebenkte sho- s'vet a poyk ton undzer trot- mir zaynen do!
Oh, yeah.
There is-- this song is in every language, in every language now.
And he was such an ordinary little-- ordinary boy, young boy.
Very-- just very-- didn't-- I don't know if he finished his school.
He was so clever.
And he was sitting in ghetto and write these songs and books.
Even books.
He had books from the ghetto.
Yes, we know him.
He was in the concentration camp with mine-- with mine-- with my husband.
If I have radio-- at home, I had.
Yes.
Not really.
No.
There wasn't such a radio like you hear today with news and things.
No, there was music.
There was not really news especially on the radio.
I never heard it anyway.
Maybe was, but I never heard.
